name: PartitionAlloc
summary: Chrome's type/size-aware partitioned allocator with BackupRefPtr (MiraclePtr) refcount-based dangling-pointer protection — the most consequential exploit-mitigation deployed at scale on a memory-unsafe codebase.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Chrome's PartitionAlloc uses type/size-aware partitioning: different "partitions" exist in disjoint address-space regions, and a freed slot can only be reused by an allocation of the same bucket *and* same partition, neutering most type-confusion exploits. PartitionAlloc-Everywhere routes all of Chrome's `malloc` to PA, so the protection covers the whole browser, not just designated security-critical code.

Paired with BackupRefPtr (also called MiraclePtr), PartitionAlloc is the most consequential exploit-mitigation deployed at scale on a memory-unsafe codebase.

sections.title: Partitioning and BackupRefPtr/MiraclePtr
sections.content:
2 MiB super-pages with permanently-inaccessible first/last partition pages act as guard pages; metadata sits in a dedicated, non-adjacent OOB region so a buffer overflow into adjacent allocations cannot rewrite allocator bookkeeping.

BackupRefPtr / MiraclePtr stores a refcount per allocation. The Chromium smart-pointer type `raw_ptr<T>` increments that refcount on construction, so a freed slot is not really released until all dangling pointers drop. The effect: a use-after-free on a `raw_ptr<T>` becomes a memory leak or a non-security crash rather than an exploitable type-confusion. Integrates with ARM MTE on Android Pixel for additional hardware-tag enforcement.

sections.title: Deployment
sections.content: Status (as of 2026-04): deployed across all Chromium-derived browsers (Chrome, Edge, Brave, Opera) reaching billions of users per public Chromium documentation; exact per-platform default scope is release-specific. MiraclePtr is the most consequential exploit-mitigation deployed at scale on a memory-unsafe codebase — it converts a category of bugs that would have been remote-code-execution into denial-of-service.
